skill.name: plugin-structure
skill.description: Guides users through Claude Code plugin directory layout, manifest configuration, component organization, and portable plugin architecture patterns.
skill.summary: Build and document Claude Code plugin structures with manifest, component, and path guidance.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review found documentation examples rather than executable behavior. The package contains Markdown guidance and JSON evaluation data, with no evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden execution, or live network access.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated external command detections are Markdown code block delimiters and inline documentation examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced Markdown examples in documentation files. No executable source file or runtime call is present.

low_findings.title: Instructional Shell Snippets Are Not Executed by the Skill
low_findings.description: Shell and Kubernetes commands appear inside example plugin documentation. They explain possible hook scripts and troubleshooting steps but are not run by this package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is documentation for plugin authors. The repository has no executable script file that invokes these commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Manifest Metadata Examples
low_findings.description: URL detections refer to sample homepage, repository, and author metadata fields in plugin manifest documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static examples in JSON manifest samples. No HTTP client, fetch call, or network request path was found.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Names Are Placeholders
low_findings.description: Credential-related detections are template placeholders that show how plugin authors can map environment variables into MCP server configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholder strings inside documented configuration examples. There is no code that reads or transmits real environment values.

low_findings.title: Hidden File and Path Traversal Detections Are Path Rules
low_findings.description: Dotfile and parent-directory detections appear in documentation for standard plugin files and path validation rules, including examples of paths to reject.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes allowed and disallowed plugin paths. It does not perform filesystem traversal or hidden file access.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Supporting Behavior
low_findings.description: The scanner matched version strings, identifiers, command names, and troubleshooting examples. Review found no cryptographic operation or reconnaissance workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are semantic version examples, troubleshooting commands, and portability advice. No hashing, encryption, scanning, or probing code exists.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Is Documentation Context
low_findings.description: The code execution, network, credential, and filesystem signals co-occur because the skill explains plugin manifests, MCP servers, hooks, and portable paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious combination is expected for documentation about plugin configuration. No obfuscation, encoded payload, or execution chain was found.

content.value_statement: Plugin authors need a reliable layout before adding commands, agents, skills, hooks, or MCP servers. This skill explains the structure and manifest patterns that make Claude Code plugins portable and maintainable.
actual_capabilities: Explains where to place .claude-plugin/plugin.json in a plugin project.
Describes standard directories for commands, agents, skills, hooks, scripts, and MCP configuration.
Documents manifest fields for metadata, component paths, hooks, and MCP servers.
Shows minimal, standard, and advanced plugin organization examples.
Explains auto-discovery behavior and portable ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} references.
Provides naming, path, and maintenance guidance for plugin authors.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not install or run plugins.
It does not verify a finished plugin against a live Claude Code runtime.
Example commands, hooks, and MCP settings must be reviewed before use.
It focuses on structure, not full business logic for a specific plugin.

use_cases.title: Plan a first plugin
use_cases.description: Learn the required manifest location, standard directories, and naming rules before creating files.

use_cases.title: Standardize internal plugins
use_cases.description: Define consistent component layouts and path conventions for plugins shared across a team.

use_cases.title: Prepare a plugin package
use_cases.description: Check structure, metadata, examples, and portability practices before sharing a plugin publicly.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me structure a new Claude Code plugin with one command and the required manifest.

prompt_templates.title: Add Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me where commands, agents, skills, hooks, and scripts should live in my plugin.

prompt_templates.title: Review Manifest Paths
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned plugin manifest paths and explain whether they follow Claude Code plugin rules.

prompt_templates.title: Design Advanced Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an advanced Claude Code plugin layout with MCP servers, hooks, shared scripts, and portable path references.

output_examples.input: I need a minimal plugin with one slash command.
output_examples.output: A concise directory plan that includes the manifest folder, a commands folder, the command file name, and naming guidance.

output_examples.input: My plugin needs hooks and an MCP server.
output_examples.output: A structured explanation of where hook configuration, hook scripts, MCP settings, and supporting files should be placed.

output_examples.input: Check whether this plugin layout is portable.
output: Notes on relative paths and disallowed parent traversal.
Suggestions to use plugin-root references for scripts.
A short list of manifest fields to verify.

best_practices: Keep the manifest in .claude-plugin and keep components at the plugin root.
Use clear kebab-case names for files, directories, commands, agents, and skills.
Review hook scripts, MCP commands, and environment variables before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not nest commands, agents, skills, or hooks inside the manifest directory.
Do not hardcode absolute local paths in plugin manifests or component files.
Do not publish example secrets or unreviewed executable hooks.

faq.question: Does this skill create plugin files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides structure and configuration guidance that an assistant can use while helping you create files.

faq.question: Where should plugin.json be located?
faq.answer: Place plugin.json inside the .claude-plugin directory at the root of the plugin.

faq.question: Can a plugin include commands, agents, skills, hooks, and MCP servers?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explains the standard locations and configuration patterns for each component type.

faq.question: Does the skill require network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation. Some examples describe MCP server configuration that a real plugin may use.

faq.question: Why does it mention environment variables?
faq.answer: It shows placeholder patterns for plugin authors who need to pass settings or credentials to MCP servers.

faq.question: Can I use this guidance with Codex and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is about Claude Code plugin structure and can be used while working with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
